sound_type: churring / chittering young -- social family-group vocalizations combining the low rolling purr of contentment (churring) and the high-pitched rapid begging calls of juvenile raccoons (chittering young), the 71st acoustic element, the first urban-associated acoustic element, the first predominantly parental-care sound in the mammalian era
ecosystem: riparian/urban -- streamside corridors, lake margins, wetland edges, floodplain forests (ancestral), plus suburban neighborhoods, urban parks, residential areas, commercial districts, and any built environment with food access (modern). The FIRST species with URBAN in its habitat description. Urban raccoon densities exceed rural by 10-20x.
season: year-round resident throughout Washington State, from sea level to mid-elevation

coordinates.description: Reference for riparian/urban habitat in the Puget Sound lowlands. Raccoons are ubiquitous across Washington from sea level to mid-elevation, inhabiting riparian corridors, urban parks, suburban neighborhoods, and commercial districts. Churring and chittering most commonly heard at night from den sites -- attics, tree cavities, chimneys -- during spring and early summer kit-rearing season.

taxonomy_note.description: Eleventh mammalian entry. Ninth mammalian species. Ninth Carnivora, first Procyonidae. NEW FAMILY (30th). BACK-TO-BACK NEW FAMILIES -- Mephitidae (29th) at degree 69, Procyonidae (30th) at degree 70. Two consecutive new families, both Carnivora, both musteloid, both omnivorous. Three musteloid families now represented: Mustelidae (63-68), Mephitidae (69), Procyonidae (70).
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Procyon from Greek pro (before) + kyon (dog) -- 'before the dog' or 'pre-dog,' referencing an early taxonomic impression that raccoons were a primitive carnivore form. lotor from Latin lotor, meaning 'washer,' referring to the raccoon's behavior of dipping food in water. Procyon lotor = 'the washing pre-dog.' Common name 'Raccoon' derives from Powhatan Algonquian aroughcun or arathkone, meaning 'animal that scratches with its hands.'
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Procyon -- two to three species: P. lotor (Raccoon), P. cancrivorus (Crab-eating Raccoon), and possibly P. pygmaeus (Cozumel Raccoon). The Raccoon is the most widespread, abundant, and studied procyonid.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Carnivora -- ninth entry. Third family represented (Mustelidae 6, Mephitidae 1, Procyonidae 1). All nine carnivore entries are musteloid (superfamily Musteloidea). No non-musteloid carnivore yet. Second consecutive omnivore in the order.
taxonomy_note.class_note: Mammalia -- eleventh mammalian entry. Ninth mammalian species. Mammalian representation rises to 15.5% (11 of 71 entries). The mammalian era (degrees 61-70) is now ten degrees deep. Two orders (Rodentia, Carnivora), five families (Castoridae, Mustelidae, Mephitidae, Procyonidae).
taxonomy_note.family_position: Procyonidae's FIRST entry at degree 70. The 30th family in the engine. NEW FAMILY. Back-to-back with Mephitidae (29th) at degree 69. Procyonidae is a New World family within the superfamily Musteloidea, containing 7 genera and approximately 14 species (raccoons, coatis, kinkajous, olingos, ringtails). Diverged from Mustelidae approximately 40-50 million years ago.

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: Churring: approximately 200-800 Hz, low-pitched rolling purr. Chittering young: approximately 1,000-6,000 Hz, high-pitched rapid repetitive calls. Wide range reflecting the two distinct vocalization types.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: Churring: 300-600 Hz (the rolling purr band). Chittering: 2,000-4,000 Hz (the high-pitched juvenile begging band).
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: NOT a song. SOCIAL FAMILY VOCALIZATIONS. Two primary components: (1) Churring -- low, rolling, purr-like contact vocalization during family rest/grooming/nursing. (2) Chittering young -- high-pitched, rapid, repetitive begging/distress calls from juvenile raccoons. Additional vocalizations include screams, growls, snarls, whimpers, trills, and purring. Over 50 distinct vocalizations documented.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Churring: sustained, 2-30+ seconds during social contact. Chittering: bursts of 1-5 seconds, repeated frequently. Family vocal sessions can persist for minutes during active kit-rearing periods.
song_metrics.note_structure: Churring: continuous rolling sound without discrete note boundaries, similar to purring. Chittering: rapid discrete notes (8-15 per second during peak agitation), each note a short, sharp, high-pitched vocalization. The two types contrast: continuous (churr) vs. discrete (chitter).
song_metrics.note_types: Churr (low, rolling, purr-like, continuous), chitter (high, rapid, discrete, repetitive), trill (rolling contact call), scream (loud aggressive), growl (close-range threat), whimper (submissive/distress). The churr/chitter combination is the Raccoon's signature -- the sound of family life.
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Churring: sustained continuous vocalization without phrase boundaries. Chittering: bursts of rapid notes separated by brief pauses, creating an insistent rhythmic pattern. Mother-kit exchanges alternate between the two: mother churrs, kits chitter in response.
song_metrics.timbre: Churring: warm, low, vibrato-like rolling quality -- similar to a large cat's purr but lower and rougher. Chittering: sharp, bright, piercing -- similar to a small bird's alarm call in pitch but with mammalian resonance. Combined: the contrast between the warm mother-sound and the sharp young-sound creates a distinctive family soundscape.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=3. Common but nocturnal. Churring is quiet (audible at 5-15 meters). Chittering is moderate (audible at 15-30 meters, carrying through building walls -- the reason homeowners discover attic raccoons). Neither is a long-distance vocalization.
song_metrics.tempo: Churring: sustained, slow, continuous. Chittering: rapid (8-15 notes per second during peak agitation). The churring calms; the chittering agitates. The tempo contrast mirrors the behavioral contrast: contentment vs. demand.
song_metrics.context: SOCIAL FAMILY COMMUNICATION. Churring: during nursing, grooming, resting in family groups. Chittering: juvenile begging for food, distress when separated from mother, excitement during exploration. Both vocalizations are nocturnal -- heard after dark from den sites (attics, tree cavities, chimneys) and foraging areas (riparian corridors, suburban yards).
song_metrics.regional_variation: NO documented regional variation in churring or chittering. The vocalizations are consistent across the species' range. Urban raccoons may vocalize more frequently due to higher population density and more frequent social interactions.
song_metrics.key_property: CHURRING / CHITTERING YOUNG -- the 71st acoustic element. Social family-group vocalizations: churring (low rolling purr of contentment) and chittering (high-pitched juvenile begging). E=3. The first URBAN-ASSOCIATED acoustic element. The first predominantly PARENTAL-CARE sound in the mammalian era. The sound of raccoon family life, heard at night from attics, tree cavities, and riparian corridors.

physical_description.overall: Large, stocky procyonid. 600-950 mm total length, 3.5-9.0 kg. The largest non-rodent mammal in the engine. Plantigrade stance (walks on soles of feet). Hunched posture with rolling gait. Robust build -- more bear-like than weasel-like. Urban raccoons tend heavier due to calorie-rich anthropogenic food.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: For degree 70, the diagnostic features are: (1) FACIAL MASK -- broad black band across the eyes, bordered by white/pale gray. The most recognizable facial marking of any PNW mammal. (2) RINGED TAIL -- bushy, with 5-7 alternating dark/light bands. (3) DEXTEROUS FOREPAWS -- five long, flexible digits. Nearly primate-like manipulation ability. (4) GRIZZLED GRAY-BROWN FUR -- dense, cryptic coloration. (5) PLANTIGRADE STANCE -- flat-footed, hunched gait.
physical_description.fur_detail: BASE: Dense, grizzled gray-brown. Thick underfur provides insulation. MASK: Broad black band across eyes, bordered by white/pale gray above and below. The facial mask is diagnostic and culturally iconic. TAIL: Bushy, ringed with 5-7 alternating bands of dark brown/black and lighter gray/brown. FUNCTION: Cryptic coloration (NOT aposematic like the Striped Skunk). The raccoon relies on nocturnal habits and dexterity, not warning coloration.
physical_description.dental_detail: 40 teeth. Less specialized carnassials than the mustelids -- reflecting the omnivorous diet. Broad, crushing molars for processing diverse food types (insects, fruit, nuts, crayfish, garbage). The dental formula accommodates the full omnivorous spectrum.
physical_description.field_id: At riparian corridors, urban parks, or suburban neighborhoods: a medium-large, stocky gray-brown mammal with a distinctive black facial mask and bushy ringed tail. Moves with a hunched, rolling gait. Primarily nocturnal. Often seen near water, garbage cans, or building entry points. When observed closely, the dexterous forepaws are immediately apparent -- the raccoon manipulates objects with hand-like precision. Distinguished from all other PNW mammals by the facial mask + ringed tail combination.

nodes.title: Procyonidae Arrives -- 30th Family, Back-to-Back New Families
nodes.description: The 30th family in the engine. NEW FAMILY. Second consecutive new family (Mephitidae 69, Procyonidae 70). Both Carnivora. Both musteloid. Both omnivores. Two new families in two degrees. Three musteloid families in eight consecutive degrees (63-70).

nodes.title: Churring / Chittering Young -- 71st Acoustic Element
nodes.description: Social family-group vocalizations: churring (low rolling purr of contentment) and chittering (high-pitched juvenile begging). E=3. The first urban-associated acoustic element. The first predominantly parental-care sound in the mammalian era. The sound of raccoon family life at night.

nodes.title: Riparian/Urban -- First Urban-Adapted Species in the Engine
nodes.description: The engine's first species with URBAN in its habitat description. Urban raccoon densities exceed rural by 10-20x. Pre-adapted for urbanism: dexterous hands, omnivorous diet, nocturnal habits, intelligence. Riparian/urban captures both ancestral streamside habitat and modern urban reality.

nodes.title: Dexterous Hands -- Most Manually Dexterous Species
nodes.description: Nearly primate-like hand dexterity. Five flexible digits, 4-5x more mechanoreceptors than most mammals, disproportionate cortical representation. Opens latches, turns doorknobs, solves multi-step puzzles. The washing behavior (Procyon lotor = 'washing pre-dog') is sensory enhancement, not hygiene. The most manually dexterous species in 71 degrees.

nodes.title: The Washing Behavior -- Procyon lotor
nodes.description: Procyon lotor = 'the washing pre-dog.' Raccoons dip food in water before eating. NOT hygiene -- wetting activates forepaw mechanoreceptors for enhanced tactile feedback. Captive raccoons dip food in dirty water, confirming sensory function. The defining behavior enters the species' scientific name and common names across languages (German Waschbar = 'wash bear').

nodes.title: Ninth Carnivore -- Third Musteloid Family
nodes.description: Carnivora grows to nine entries across three families: Mustelidae (6), Mephitidae (1), Procyonidae (1). All nine entries are musteloid (superfamily Musteloidea). No non-musteloid carnivore yet. Second consecutive omnivore in the order. Third consecutive non-hypercarnivore.

nodes.title: Second Consecutive Omnivore in Carnivora
nodes.description: Like the Striped Skunk (69), the Raccoon is classified in Carnivora but is functionally omnivorous. The second consecutive Carnivora omnivore. Third consecutive non-hypercarnivore. The order name describes ancestry, not diet -- demonstrated in two consecutive entries.

nodes.title: Nocturnal -- Strictly Night-Active in Urban Settings
nodes.description: The engine's most consistently nocturnal mammal. Strictly night-active in urban settings -- emerging after full darkness, returning before dawn. Second nocturnal-primary species in the mammalian era after beaver chewing (62). More nocturnal than the skunk. Eight nocturnal-primary species in the engine.

nodes.title: Tenth Consecutive Original CSV Species -- A DECADE
nodes.description: Degrees 61-70: TEN consecutive original CSV species. A full decade of originals. The longest original-CSV streak in engine history. Every mammalian entry has been an original CSV species. No substitutions needed or considered. Ten in a row -- a milestone.

nodes.title: Eleventh Mammalian Entry -- The Mammalian Era at Ten Degrees
nodes.description: Eleven mammalian entries across ten degrees (61-70). Nine mammalian species. Two orders (Rodentia, Carnivora), five families (Castoridae, Mustelidae, Mephitidae, Procyonidae). Mammalian representation rises to 15.5% (11 of 71 entries). The mammalian era is ten degrees deep. EXTREME imbalance.

nodes.title: The Horse Feathers Parallel
nodes.description: Both adaptable (Horse Feathers moved between cities; raccoons thrive everywhere). Both dexterous (intricate multi-instrument arrangements; intricate hands). Both nocturnal (folk music is evening music; raccoons are night animals). E=3 meets E=3 -- perfectly matched energy. Four parallel dimensions.

cross_references.relationship: CONSECUTIVE NEW FAMILIES. Mephitidae (69) then Procyonidae (70). Both Carnivora omnivores. Both nocturnal. Both musteloid. Back-to-back new family introductions -- two degrees, two new families.
BOTH URBAN-ADAPTED AND NOCTURNAL. Both dexterous -- beaver with incisors, raccoon with hands. Both have colonized urban waterways. Both common, both resident. Day-engineering vs. night-foraging.
BOTH URBAN GENERALISTS. Park pond neighbors -- Mallard on the water by day, raccoon on the bank by night. Both thrive in cities. Both ubiquitous in urban PNW parks and waterways.

BOTH UBIQUITOUS, BOTH FAMILIAR. Day vs. night equivalents. The robin is the most recognized diurnal species; the raccoon is the most recognized nocturnal species. Complementary familiarity across the 24-hour cycle.
BOTH NOCTURNAL. Forest specialist vs. urban generalist. The owl hunts old-growth darkness; the raccoon raids suburban darkness. Opposite ends of the nocturnal spectrum -- wilderness vs. city.
Eleventh mammalian entry. Ninth mammalian species. Ninth Carnivora, first Procyonidae. Mammalian representation rises to 15.5% (11 of 71 entries). Two orders, five families, nine species.

Riparian/urban habitat. The first urban-adapted species in the engine. Omnivorous diet spanning aquatic invertebrates to garbage. Dexterous manipulation. Nocturnal ecology. The bridge between wild PNW and built PNW.
